title: skopeo copies images/layers that are already present (docker-daemon -> docker-daemon)
body:
I'm using Skopeo to copy from the local docker-daemon to a docker-daemon on another machine, which is accessed via an ssh-forwarded unix socket. I believe skopeo should skip copying the images/layers that are already present on the destination, but it does not appear to.

I've tried both the version of Skopeo that's packaged in Ubuntu 22.04, and git HEAD (I compiled it on Ubuntu 23.04, but ran it on Ubuntu 22.04). The results were the same. The debug log below is from git HEAD.

I can see that some large images/layers/... are copied during this process, even if I simply re-run the exact same command over and over without changing the Docker image at all. This shows up in both skopeo's output (gives a progress bar and a bandwidth readout) and via `iotop` on the destination machine (shows high bandwidth use).

Machine A (destination):

```
ssh -R /swarren-data/machinea.docker.sock:/run/docker.sock machineb
```

Machine B (source):

```
skopeo copy \
    --debug \  # log is below
    --dest-precompute-digests \ # same issue with or without this with git HEAD; option doesn't exist on Ubuntu 22.04's version
    --dest-daemon-host unix:///swarren-data/machinea.docker.sock \
    docker-daemon:srcimage:srctag \
    docker-daemon:dstimage:dsttag
```

--debug Log:

comments.body:
Thanks for reaching out.

Yes; the `docker-daemon` transport is a wrapper around one-shot `docker save`/`docker load`, it is not interactive in a way that would allow detecting that layers already exist. It is an interoperability/transition mechanism, so significant changes to add new features are not very likely.

I would rather strongly recommend not using `docker-daemon` in workflows, _especially not_ two of them: creating/consuming the `docker-daemon` tarballs, one uncompressed file at a time, is always going to be _much_ slower, CPU and IO-intensive, than doing registry-to-registry copies (`docker://`→`docker://`) of compressed data.

> It is an interoperability/transition mechanism, so significant changes to add new features are not very likely.

Even if we wanted to, this is also just impossible: The Docker Engine API does not provide any way to export a single layer, nor a way to import a single layer. So, at best, we could `docker save`, detect that some layers do not exist at the destination, and create an _invalid_ archive with missing layers which `docker load` can consume because it sees the locally-available layers. I have heard of someone building such invalid archives, but that‘s a hack that can break at any time if the `docker load` implementation ever changes.

> Even if we wanted to, this is also just impossible:
> The Docker Engine API does not provide any way to export a single layer, nor a way to import a single layer

I was worried you were going to say that.

> I have heard of someone building such invalid archives, but that‘s a hack that can break at any time if the
> docker load implementation ever changes.

Interesting. I was vaguely thinking of trying that before finding Skopeo... I don't suppose this was in a published script/tool anywhere?

I don't suppose there are any Docker implementations (or container systems in general) that use some custom daemon that shares storage between a Docker daemon and Docker register implementation, to avoid the duplicate-disk-space usage of a push/pull model?

Feel free to close this bug since there isn't anything Skopeo can do about this. Thanks.

> > I have heard of someone building such invalid archives, but that‘s a hack that can break at any time if the
> > docker load implementation ever changes.
> 
> Interesting. I was vaguely thinking of trying that before finding Skopeo... I don't suppose this was in a published script/tool anywhere?

I vaguely remember a GitHub Issue discussing that somewhere in the c/image or Skopeo repo, or possibly elsewhere in GitHub.com/containers, but I’m afraid I can’t find this now.

> I don't suppose there are any Docker implementations (or container systems in general) that use some custom daemon that shares storage between a Docker daemon and Docker register implementation

That’s just not possible; the daemon must contain uncompressed filesystems that can be executed against, while the registry contains a linear tarball, and preferably a compressed one.

FWIW, the "invalid archive" approach seems to work for now...

https://github.com/swarren/misc-public-scripts/blob/main/docker-push-to-remote-daemon.sh

> That’s just not possible

I'm not entirely sure yet:-) Not that it really matters; this is just some idle thoughts:-)

> the daemon must contain uncompressed filesystems that can be executed against,

Certainly.

> while the registry contains a linear tarball, and preferably a compressed one.

Is the registry protocol defined so that that the registry server _must_ store linear/compressed tarballs, or only that it is able to send/receive these over the network? I.e. could the registry server untar on RX and tar on TX? While this would be inefficient for a regular registry server, if this was a special registry server with the sole use-case of interfacing with the pool of images used by the docker daemon for local development, that would probably be OK, since the registry protocol wouldn't be the primary access mechanism.
Rootful podman might be able to work with such a registry  via a network file system protocol like NFS without needing to pull the images using Additional Image Stores.

What @rhatdan says — I think the design would benefit from being discussed at a higher level of abstraction than “we know we are going to use Docker, and we know we are going to use the unpackaged data underlying the graph driver as the primary storage”. Either using a registry, like most other users, or using a network filesystem to share the unpackaged images, could be on the table.

---

(It doesn’t necessarily have to _store_ tarballs but it must know and compute SHA256 digests of those tarballs, i.e. they must be byte-for-byte exactly reproducible. The storage underlying Docker and/or Podman can do that _for uncompressed tarballs_; compressed tarballs can only be reproduced by storing them, never by reproducing them.)
